serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of nasi goreng ayam kunyit as typically served at a Singapore hawker stall or eating house, approximately 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Goreng (Fried Rice with Egg) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo provides nutrient data for nasi goreng (fried rice with egg). Values have been adapted to account for the addition of chicken and turmeric, which modestly increases protein and fat content. The base fried rice macros are anchored to HPB data; chicken contribution estimated from ICMR-NIN cooked chicken values (per 100 g composite). The composite per-100 g values represent a typical nasi goreng ayam kunyit as served at a Singapore Indian-Muslim eating house.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried rice
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for nasi goreng ayam kunyit. GI of 54 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white fried rice (GI ~54, tested value from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables). Turmeric has been shown in peer-reviewed literature to modestly attenuate postprandial glycaemia; the base fried rice GI of 54 is therefore a conservative estimate. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 85.75) / 100 ≈ 47.2, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 24.5 × 3.5 = 85.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~1330 mg per standard 350 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-salt preparation, as a single serving can contribute approximately 58% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~54) and high glycemic load per serving (~47) due to large carbohydrate portion from white rice
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion (150–200 g) to limit glycemic load, and may benefit from pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response; the turmeric content may offer modest anti-inflammatory benefit.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~1.4 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium; turmeric (curcumin) may confer cardioprotective anti-inflammatory effects
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the sodium and saturated fat content per serving; choosing lean chicken breast and minimising added oil during preparation can reduce cardiovascular risk, while the turmeric component may provide modest anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Goreng Ayam Kunyit is a turmeric-spiced chicken fried rice dish popular in Singapore's Indian-Muslim (Mamak) and Malay culinary traditions. The dish is prepared by stir-frying pre-cooked white rice in a hot wok with marinated chicken pieces, turmeric powder, garlic, onion, chilli, egg, and soy sauce, resulting in a fragrant, golden-yellow rice with a mildly spiced, savoury flavour and a slightly firm, wok-charred texture. The name translates literally as 'fried rice with turmeric chicken' — 'nasi' (rice), 'goreng' (fried), 'ayam' (chicken), 'kunyit' (turmeric). It is commonly served as a one-dish meal at hawker centres, coffee shops (kopitiams), and Indian-Muslim eating houses across Singapore, typically accompanied by a fried egg, cucumber slices, and sambal chilli. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g plate provides approximately 612 kcal, 27 g protein, 18 g fat, and 86 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is elevated (~1330 mg per serving), making portion control important for individuals with hypertension. The glycemic index is approximately 54 (low-moderate), though the glycemic load per serving is high (~47) due to the large rice portion, which is clinically relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. Turmeric contains curcumin, a bioactive compound with documented anti-inflammatory and potential insulin-sensitising properties, which may offer modest metabolic benefit. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore establishments and is dairy-free.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists for nasi goreng ayam kunyit specifically. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo nasi goreng (fried rice with egg) data with chicken and turmeric contribution estimated from ICMR-NIN cooked chicken values. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database fried rice entry. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. The input food name 'Nasi Goreng Ayam Kunit' contains a likely spelling variant of 'kunyit' (turmeric in Malay/Indonesian); the canonical spelling 'Kunyit' is used in the output food_name. The variant 'Kunit' is preserved in aliases. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source database for this food category. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source database for this food category. 4. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked chicken and rice composite data as HPB does not report these fractions for this dish. 5. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fried rice entry; no direct GI measurement for nasi goreng ayam kunyit was found in approved sources. 6. The cuisine tag 'Noodles' and 'Drinks & Beverages' in the input are not applicable to this dish and have been disregarded; the dish is correctly classified as a fried rice preparation under Indian/Mamak cuisine. 7. The input listed cuisine as 'Indian' which is retained as this dish is served at an Indian-Muslim eating house (Baba Eating House, South Bridge Road, Singapore).
